Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly spending around 30 hours a week on coursework. I'm a visual learner, so I'd block out time to watch videos and create my own diagrams and mind maps. I usually studied in the evenings after work since that's when I could focus best. I had a system going, but looking back, maybe I wasn't focusing on the right things.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but life got in the way more than I'd like to admit. Being 26 with limited resources means I had to juggle work and other responsibilities. When deadlines approached, I'd definitely shift my schedule around, but I tried to maintain some consistency with my 31 hours each week. The problem was that my attendance in live sessions was basically non-existent because of scheduling conflicts.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check for new video content or to see if there were discussion threads I could participate in. I also wanted to keep track of any announcements or deadline reminders. Sometimes I'd just browse to see what materials were available, even if I wasn't planning to dive deep that day. I logged in pretty frequently, probably 43 times over the course, but I'm not sure how productive each session was.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period where logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, check my phone, and open the LMS before even having breakfast. It happened because I genuinely wanted to stay on top of the video content and discussions. But honestly, routine doesn't always equal effectiveness, and I think I fell into that trap.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I watched over 140 minutes of video content and really tried to absorb everything through that medium. The discussion posts were also helpful because engaging with peers helped me process ideas differently. I participated in about 30 peer interactions which felt meaningful at the time.
Q6: I definitely underutilized the reading materials and text-based resources. My brain just doesn't process written content as efficiently, so I'd skim or skip entirely. The assignments were something I struggled with too, only completing about 29% of them. I kept telling myself I'd catch up, but other priorities kept pushing them down my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate visual results or when it connected concepts in a way I could picture. Discussion posts felt worthwhile because I could interact with others and test my understanding. If something felt abstract or purely text-based without any visual component, I'd struggle to justify spending time on it when I had limited hours available.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford to indulge in. With limited resources and time constraints, I had to prioritize what was required. But ironically, sometimes the optional stuff was more engaging because there was no pressure attached. Required activities felt heavier, like obligations rather than learning opportunities.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that the LMS would never capture. I'd download videos and watch them on my commute or during breaks at work. I created physical flashcards and visual diagrams that I'd review constantly. A lot of my actual learning happened with a notebook and colored pens, mapping out concepts visually away from any screen.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd study from printed materials or my own handwritten notes for hours. I'd also discuss course concepts with a friend who was in a similar field, which helped me understand things better. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best learning moments often happened when I was drawing connections on paper, not clicking through the LMS.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd access a video once, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline over the following days. With my limited internet reliability and resources, downloading made more sense than streaming repeatedly. So the LMS would show one view, but I might have watched that same content five or six times on my own device.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My low resilience meant that when things got overwhelming, I'd shut down rather than push through. Work demands would spike, and suddenly I couldn't maintain my study routine. Those were the weeks where my attendance and assignment completion really suffered.
Q13: Workload from outside the course definitely impacted my LMS usage. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just passively scroll rather than actively engage. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections entirely sometimes. Having limited resources meant I couldn't always access supplementary materials that might have helped clarify things.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered elsewhere. Sometimes I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but not actually processing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine learning. My brain was present but my focus wasn't.
Q15: The assignments were my biggest avoidance area. I knew they were important, but the anxiety of starting them combined with my low resilience made me procrastinate constantly. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first before attempting them. That perfectionism, or maybe fear of failure, meant I only completed about 29% of what I should have.
Q16: Deadlines created this frantic energy where I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS. I'd cram in video watching and try to catch up on discussions right before something was due. But that reactive approach meant I wasn't engaging deeply, just trying to check boxes. The deadline-driven behavior probably inflated my login numbers without improving my actual learning.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got positive responses on discussion posts, I felt motivated to participate more. But feedback on assignments, when I did submit them, sometimes felt discouraging given my low scores. The CBT sessions I attended helped me reframe some of that negative self-talk, but it was an ongoing struggle to not let poor feedback derail me completely.
Q18: The course structure with its heavy emphasis on assignments didn't play to my strengths as a visual learner. I thrived with the video content but struggled with the written submission requirements. The flexibility of 20 online courses sounds great, but it also meant I had to self-regulate heavily, which wasn't my strong suit. More structured visual activities would have helped me.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement easier for someone with my resource limitations. However, the assignment-heavy grading structure made things harder since that's where I consistently fell short. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged participation, which worked well for me. But the lack of visual assessment options really disadvantaged my learning style.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty accurately, actually. I was active in the LMS, watching videos, participating in discussions, logging in frequently. But my assignment submissions were terrible, under 30%. Sometimes you're engaged with learning the content but paralyzed when it comes to producing formal work. The fear of judgment or failure can create this disconnect between consumption and output.
Q21: That's almost the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some people are efficient, they access what they need, study offline, and perform well on assessments. They don't need to be constantly logged in to succeed. My high activity didn't translate to performance, which suggests that engagement metrics don't tell the whole story about learning effectiveness.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, frequency, and time spent on different resources. They capture participation in discussions and submission rates accurately. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually thinking critically or just scrolling. They also miss all offline learning, emotional states, and the real barriers students face outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions, making connections, and caring about understanding. It's not just showing up or clicking through content. For me, engagement happened when I was drawing diagrams and discussing concepts with peers, not necessarily when I was logged into the LMS. True engagement is mental and emotional investment in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my extensive offline visual studying, the hours with colored pens and diagrams. It wouldn't capture my conversations with friends about course concepts or the mental energy I invested despite my struggles. It also wouldn't show how my work schedule and limited resources constrained my choices. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that I genuinely wanted to succeed but couldn't translate that into the required outputs.
